voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: South America — tropical forests, savanna, and semi-desert scrub
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its forked tongue flicks to sample chemical particles from air and ground.
Its nose detects prey underground and beneath leaf litter.
Its eyes pick up movement across a wide area around its body.
It senses vibrations through the ground from nearby animals.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in tropical forest edges, savanna, and scrubland across South America.
It digs deep burrows for shelter, brumation in winter, and egg laying.
Warm, open ground provides the sun exposure it needs to regulate body temperature.
It needs access to both open ground for basking and dense cover for shelter.
How does it send messages?: It hisses loudly as a warning when it feels cornered.
Males leave scent trails from glands during breeding season to attract females.
Body puffing and slow tail-waving signal aggression to rival males.
Female tegus follow scent trails more precisely than males during mating season.
What does it do when something changes?: When threatened, it may flee quickly or stand its ground and hiss.
It enters brumation in its burrow during cold winter months and does not feed.
During breeding season, its body temperature rises several degrees above the burrow temperature.
Disturbed tegus will retreat to their burrow entrance and watch from safety.
How does it learn?: Young tegus hatch independently and must find food and shelter on their own.
They learn through experience which prey items are accessible and safe to pursue.
Juvenile tegus use tongue-flicking to gather information about their new environment constantly.
How does it have babies?: A female lays a clutch of eggs inside a burrow or termite mound.
She guards the nest and regulates its temperature with her own body heat.
Hatchlings break out of their eggs independently and must fend for themselves.
Young tegus are vulnerable to predators until they grow large enough.
What are its limits?: Cold temperatures shut down its activity and force it into the burrow for months.
Without warm basking spots it cannot digest food or move effectively.
It needs eggs, insects, and small animals to fuel its high activity level.
Habitat loss removes the warm, open areas it needs for foraging and basking.
What does this teach us?: During mating season, it can warm its own body without sunlight.
Its tongue reads the world constantly — always gathering, always testing.
Activity, digestion, and reproduction are all tied directly to temperature.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Enters a seasonal low-activity period in the burrow during cold months.
Can raise body temperature several degrees above ambient during breeding season.
Females actively guard nests and regulate nest temperature.
Omnivorous — eats eggs, insects, small vertebrates, fruit, and carrion.
still_debated: How widespread facultative endothermy is across the year, not just breeding.
How strongly scent trails carry identity vs. simple location information.
Invasive-population dynamics in areas outside its native range.
common_failure_modes: Calling it cold-blooded in the simple sense — breeding-season physiology is more complex.
Treating it as a monitor lizard — tegus are teiids, not varanids.
Ignoring its impact where it has been introduced outside South America.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe it as a monitor lizard.
Do not claim it is fully ectothermic year-round without qualification.
Do not ignore invasive-population concerns when discussing distribution.
